Member-only story

9 min read
Just now

--

Picture two web pages with identical content — same title, same text, same images. One is built using <div> for everything. The other uses the right HTML elements: <header>, <nav>, <main>, <article>, <footer>. Which one is easier for a search engine to understand?
The answer is obvious — yet a surprising number of developers still write HTML like the first example, unknowingly making it harder for Google to make sense of their pages.
You can read the full story for free by clicking here
In 2026, search engines don’t just read text. They understand structure, context, and meaning. Semantic HTML is how we communicate directly with crawlers — telling them what’s important, what’s navigation, and what’s the main content. The result: better rankings, more frequent rich snippets, and pages that are genuinely accessible to everyone.
What Is Semantic HTML?
Semantic HTML means using HTML elements that describe the meaning of content, not just its appearance. Instead of wrapping everything in <div> and <span>, you use elements like <header>, <nav>, <article>, <section>, and <footer> that explicitly declare the role of each part.
